The Son of God here assures his church of his unchanging love, and his pleading for her under all trials and difficulties. She will be called by a new name, a pleasing name, such as she was never called by before. Before the preaching of the gospel, no one seemed to have any real concern for the state of true religion in the world. God, by his grace, has wrought that in his church, which makes her his delight. Let us learn motives to holiness from this. If the Lord rejoices over us, we should rejoice in his service.

God’s professing people must be a praying people. He is not displeased with us for being earnest, as people commonly are; he bids us to cry after him and give him no rest. It is a sign that God is coming to a people in mercy when he pours out a spirit of prayer upon them. See how uncertain our earthly comforts are. See also God’s mercy in giving plenty and peace to enjoy it. Let us delight in attending the courts of the Lord, so we may enjoy the consolations of his Spirit.

A way will be made for Christ's salvation; all difficulties will be removed. He brings a reward of comfort and peace with him, but a work of humiliation and reformation before him; and they will be called, The holy people, and, The redeemed of the Lord. Holiness puts honor and beauty upon any place or person, makes them admired, beloved, and sought after. Many events may have been partial fulfillments of this, as pledges of more glorious times yet to come. The close connection between the blessedness of the Jews and of the Gentiles runs through the Scriptures. The Lord Jesus will complete his work, and he never will forsake one whom he has redeemed and sanctified.
